content.full_text:
Peter R. Chacon, Chairman                                    Barbara Mihnan
William Baker                                                         Chief Consultant
Tom Bane
Dave Elder                          Assembly                          Luisa Menhaca
Philip Isenberg                                                       Principal Consultant
Johan Klehs                          California Legislature            Victoria Long
Ted Lempert                                                           Associate Consultant
Richard Mountjoy
                                     PETER R. CHACON, CHAIRMAN         Connie Delgado
Curt Pringle                                                          Committee Secretary
Maxine Waters                    Committee on Elections, Reapportionment
Paul Woodruff                                                         State Capitol
                                 and Constitutional Amendments        P.O. Box 942849
                                                                      Sacramento, CA 94249-0001
                                                                      (916) 322-5249


February 2, 1990

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, 8th Floor
Sacramento, California  95814

Dear Ms. Donovan:

     I am writing concerning the requirement of Section 81012 of the
Government Code that, for any bill amending the Political Reform
Act, "at least 12 days prior to passage in each house the bill in
its final form has been delivered to the Commission...". A question
has arisen as to the proper interpretation of this 12 day notice
requirement.

     Government Code Section 6800 provides:

          The time in which any act provided by law
          is to be done is computed by excluding the
          first day, and including the last, unless
          the last day is a holiday, and then it is
          also excluded.

     The same language is found in Civil Code Section 10, and in the
Code of Civil Procedure Section 12.

     Since there is no language in Section 81012 establishing a
different method of counting the 12 day period, the method in
Section 6800 is the one that should be applied.  Clements v.
Pasadena Finance Co. (1967) 376 F2d 1005 ("In order to apply a
different method of computing time, the particular statute
involved must specifically require the application of a
different rule." [citations omitted])

Kathryn Donovan
February 1, 1990
Page 2


Applying Section 6800 to the 12 day requirement, the first day
would be the day on which the Legislature delivers the bill to
the commission. That day would be excluded from the
calculation. The last day would be the day on which the floor
vote on passage of the bill could be taken. That day would be
included in calculating the 12 day period. Thus, if the Clerk
of the Assembly were to deliver a bill in its final form to the
FPPC on February 1, the Assembly could vote on passage of that
bill on February 13.

This is the way that Section 6800 has been applied in other
situations involving notice requirements similar to that in
Section 81012. In Clements v. Pasadena Finance Co., supra,
recordation of notice was required "at least 10 days before the
date of the transfer." Notice was recorded on August 10 at
3:30 p.m.; a transfer on August 20 at 10:00 a.m. was held to be
valid.*

The courts have specifically declined to interpret a statutory
notice requirement to mean that the full notice period must
occur after the day of the first event (in this case, the day
the bill is delivered to the commission), and before the day of
the second event (the day of the vote on the bill). "Only the
exclusion of either the first day or the last day is required, not
both." Clements v. Pasadena Finance Co., supra.

In light of these cases, the Assembly has determined that it
will apply Government Code Section 6800 to the 12 day notice
requirement in Section 81012. In addition, we will calculate
the 12 days in the same way as the courts have calculated
similar notice requirements in other statutes.

Please let me know if you have any questions about our
calculation of the 12 day notice requirement.

Sincerely,


PETER R. CHACON

PRC:cd

* The court in Clements referred to several California cases
interpreting similar requirements (for example, a notice posted

Kathryn Donovan
February 1, 1990
Page 3

on July 12 was sufficient for a hearing on July 22, when the
statute required posting of the notice "at least 10 days before
hearing"). The statutory language interpreted in Clemdis was
almost identical to the language in Section 81012 ("at least 12
days prior to passage"). See also City of Pleasanton v. Bryant,
(1965) 408 P2d 135 ("a period of 90 days" after January 18 ends
on April 18; a provision for hearing objections "at least 20
days after the posting of notices" means a hearing can be held
on October 16 when notice is posted September 25 [citation
omitted]); Harris v. Alcoholic Beverages Control Board (1963)
223 CA2d 562, 571 (appeal required to be filed "on or before
the 10th day" after a specified event; the event occurred on
January 16; the last day for the appeal was January 26).

sections.question_synthetic: How should the 12-day notice requirement in Government Code Section 81012 (requiring bills amending the Political Reform Act be delivered to the FPPC at least 12 days prior to passage) be properly calculated?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/correspondence from the Assembly to FPPC, not an FPPC advice letter response. The document explains the Assembly's interpretation of statutory notice requirements rather than requesting FPPC legal advice. No formal Question and Conclusion sections present. Dated February 1-2, 1990 with some OCR errors in header text.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: How should the 12-day notice requirement in Government Code Section 81012 (requiring bills amending the Political Reform Act be delivered to the FPPC at least 12 days prior to passage) be properly calculated?

embedding.summary: This is a letter from Assembly Chairman Peter R. Chacon to FPPC General Counsel Kathryn Donovan explaining the Assembly's interpretation of the 12-day notice requirement in Government Code Section 81012, applying Government Code Section 6800's time calculation method (excluding the first day, including the last day) based on case law precedent.
